Job maintained that God’s actions are governed by the highest wisdom. To confirm this, he showed how much knowledge and wealth people can acquire for themselves. The caverns of the earth may be discovered, but not the plans of Heaven. Go to the miners, you who are lazy in your faith, consider their ways, and be wise. Let their courage and diligence in seeking temporary wealth shame us out of laziness and faint-heartedness in working for true riches. How much better it is to gain wisdom than gold! How much easier, and safer! Yet gold is sought after, but grace is neglected. Will the hopes of valuable things from the earth, which men call them, though they are really worthless and temporary, be such a spur to hard work, and will not the certain promise of truly valuable things in heaven be much more so?

Job here speaks of wisdom and understanding, knowing and enjoying God and ourselves. Its worth is infinitely greater than all the riches in this world. It is a gift from the Holy Spirit which cannot be bought with money. Let what is most precious in God’s eyes also be precious to us. Job asks about it as one who truly desired to find it, and despaired of finding it anywhere but in God; any way except by divine revelation.

There is a two-fold wisdom: one hidden in God, which is secret, and does not belong to us; the other made known by him, and revealed to man. One day's events, and one man’s affairs, have such connection to each other that He only, to whom all is open, and who sees the whole at once, can rightly judge of every part. But the knowledge of God’s revealed will is within our reach, and will benefit us. Let people look upon this as their wisdom: To fear the Lord, and to turn away from evil. Let them learn that, and they are learned enough. Where is this wisdom to be found? The treasures of it are hidden in Christ, revealed by the word, received by faith, through the Holy Spirit. It will not feed pride or vanity, or satisfy our empty curiosity. It teaches and encourages sinners to fear the Lord, and to turn away from evil, in the practice of repentance and faith, without wanting to solve all the difficulties about the events of this life.
